name: völvas
shortDescription: Völvas were seeresses and prophetesses in Norse society, highly respected for their ability to see the future and communicate with the spirit world.
description: Völvas (singular völva) were powerful female figures in Norse society, acting as seeresses, prophetesses, and practitioners of magic (seiðr). Their role was deeply intertwined with the religious and social fabric of the Viking Age. Völvas were believed to possess the ability to see into the future, communicate with spirits, and influence events through their magical practices. They were often consulted by both commoners and leaders for guidance, advice, and to uncover hidden knowledge. The practice of seiðr, which völvas were particularly adept at, involved trance-like states, chanting, and the use of ritual objects to manipulate fate and the unseen world. While men could practice forms of magic, seiðr was primarily associated with women, and male practitioners were often viewed with suspicion or even disdain. Völvas held significant social status, often traveling from place to place and being received with honor and respect. Their pronouncements and prophecies carried considerable weight, influencing decisions and shaping the beliefs of the community. The accounts of völvas are primarily found in Norse sagas and poems, such as the Völuspá in the Poetic Edda, which details the creation and destruction of the world as foreseen by a völva. The disappearance of the völva figure coincides with the Christianization of Scandinavia, suggesting a suppression of their traditional roles and beliefs.

sources.title: The Viking Age: A Reader
sources.author: Angus A. Somerville and R. Andrew McDonald
